message.content:
Implementation files: src/game-of-life.ts
Test files: src/game-of-life.spec.ts
Passing tests: 9

Run the final metric-driven refactoring pass over the whole src/.
Iterate ONE change at a time with pre/post measurement (ESLint, cognitive,
APP, McCabe). Stop when no metric improves further or no improvement is
possible. Return after completion.

attachment.content:
- green: TDD Green Phase
- red: TDD Red Phase
- test-list: TDD Test List Phase
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text: All 9 tests pass. Let me run ESLint for PRE-measurement.

input.command: pnpm exec eslint src/ --format json 2>&1 | tail -200
input.description: Run ESLint with JSON output for PRE-measurement

content.text:
ESLint reports **zero smells** in the production file. Now let me compute APP mass and McCabe for each function.

## Iteration 1 — PRE Measurements

### ESLint Smells (whole src/): 0

### Naming Evaluation

Functions in `src/game-of-life.ts`:
- `cellKey` — purpose: stringify a cell coordinate for set/map keys. Name is clear.
- `neighborsOf` — purpose: generate the 8 neighbor coordinates of a cell. Name is clear.
- `uniqueCells` — purpose: dedupe cells by coordinate. Name is clear.
- `nextGeneration` — purpose: compute next generation of Game of Life. Name is clear.
- `isLive` — purpose: check if cell is alive. Name is clear.
- `countLiveNeighbors` — purpose: count live neighbors of a cell. Name is clear.
- `isAliveNextGeneration` — purpose: decide if cell is alive next generation. Name is clear.

All names accurately describe their purpose.

### APP Mass Calculation

Let me carefully count each function:

**`cellKey`** (line 12): `([x, y]: Cell): string => \`${x},${y}\``
- Bindings: `x`, `y` (2)
- Template literal with 2 interpolations: minimal — 2 bindings inside template
- Mass: 2 (params) + 2 (interpolations as bindings) = ~4

**`neighborsOf`** (line 14-15):
- Bindings: `x`, `y` (params), `dx`, `dy` (inner params) = 4
- Invocations: `NEIGHBOR_OFFSETS.map` = 1 → +2
- Bindings/uses: `x+dx`, `y+dy` (4 uses)
- Mass: 4 (bindings) + 2 (invocation) + 4 (binding uses in arithmetic) ≈ 10

**`uniqueCells`** (line 17-18):
- Bindings: `cells` (1)
- Invocations: `new Map`, `cells.map`, `cellKey`, `.values`, spread = 5 → +10
- Mass: ~12

**`nextGeneration`** (lines 20-40):
- Bindings: `liveCells`, `liveSet` = 2
- Assignments: `const liveSet`, `const candidates` = 2 → +12
- Invocations: `new Set(...)`, `liveCells.map(cellKey)`, `uniqueCells(...)`, spread, `liveCells.flatMap(neighborsOf)`, `candidates.filter(...)` = 6 → +12
- Mass: ~26 (for the main function body)

**`isLive`** (inner): bindings 1, invocations `liveSet.has`, `cellKey` = 2 → +4. Mass ~6

**`countLiveNeighbors`** (inner): invocations `neighborsOf`, `.filter`, `.length` = 3 → +6. Mass ~8

**`isAliveNextGeneration`** (inner, lines 32-37):
- Bindings: `cell`, `liveNeighbors`, `reproduces`, `survives` = 4
- Assignments: 3 const = +18
- Conditionals: `reproduces || survives` = `||` is a conditional → +4
- Invocations: `countLiveNeighbors`, `isLive` = 2 → +4
- Comparisons: `===` (×2) — these are bindings/operators
- Mass: ~30

### McCabe Cyclomatic Complexity

- `cellKey`: 1 (no branches)
- `neighborsOf`: 1 (no branches)
- `uniqueCells`: 1 (no branches)
- `nextGeneration` (top-level): 1 (no branches)
- `isLive` (inner): 1
- `countLiveNeighbors` (inner): 1
- `isAliveNextGeneration` (inner): 2 (has `||`)

| File | Function | APP Mass | McCabe |
|------|----------|----------|--------|
| game-of-life.ts | cellKey | 4 | 1 |
| game-of-life.ts | neighborsOf | 10 | 1 |
| game-of-life.ts | uniqueCells | 12 | 1 |
| game-of-life.ts | nextGeneration | 26 | 1 |
| game-of-life.ts | isLive (inner) | 6 | 1 |
| game-of-life.ts | countLiveNeighbors (inner) | 8 | 1 |
| game-of-life.ts | isAliveNextGeneration (inner) | 30 | 2 ← highest |

### Analysis

- ESLint smells: **0** (clean)
- Max McCabe: **2** (only `isAliveNextGeneration` has any branching, via `||`)
- Max APP mass: **30** (`isAliveNextGeneration` — but this is small)
- All names are clear and self-documenting

The worst offender is `isAliveNextGeneration` with McCabe=2 and APP=30. Let me look for potential improvements.

Looking at the function:
```typescript
const isAliveNextGeneration = (cell: Cell): boolean => {
  const liveNeighbors = countLiveNeighbors(cell);
  const reproduces = liveNeighbors === NEIGHBORS_TO_REPRODUCE;
  const survives = liveNeighbors === NEIGHBORS_TO_SURVIVE && isLive(cell);
  return reproduces || survives;
};
```

The `&&` adds another decision point that I missed in my count. Let me recount McCabe: starts at 1, +1 for `&&`, +1 for `||` = **3**.

Recomputing table:

| Function | APP Mass | McCabe |
|----------|----------|--------|
| isAliveNextGeneration | 30 | 3 ← highest |

Could we simplify it? Options:
1. **Inline assignments** — fewer bindings, lower mass, but readability suffers.
2. **Restructure conditionally**: e.g., a single expression based on count. But the `&&` for `isLive` is essential — survival requires being alive AND having 2 neighbors.

Let me consider: when a cell has 3 live neighbors, it's alive next gen regardless of current state. When it has 2 neighbors, it's alive next gen only if currently alive. Otherwise dead.

This can be expressed more directly:
```typescript
const isAliveNextGeneration = (cell: Cell): boolean => {
  const n = countLiveNeighbors(cell);
  return n === NEIGHBORS_TO_REPRODUCE || (n === NEIGHBORS_TO_SURVIVE && isLive(cell));
};
```

This removes 2 of the 3 assignments. McCabe stays at 3 (still `||` and `&&`). APP mass would drop significantly (fewer assignments).

But wait — Rule 2 (Reveals Intent) trumps APP. Are `reproduces` and `survives` more or less revealing than the inline expression?

Naming `reproduces` and `survives` is quite intent-revealing — they map directly to Game of Life concepts (Conway's rules). Removing them sacrifices clarity for fewer elements.

Let me think about other refactoring angles.

Actually, looking more carefully — the variables `reproduces` and `survives` are great names that capture domain semantics. **This is exactly the kind of clarity the Four Rules prioritize over APP minimization.**

Let me consider whether there's a way to reduce McCabe without losing the named domain concepts. We could extract helpers:

```typescript
const reproduces = (n: number) => n === NEIGHBORS_TO_REPRODUCE;
const survives = (n: number, alive: boolean) => alive && n === NEIGHBORS_TO_SURVIVE;
```

But this just relocates the McCabe count — overall complexity stays the same.

Let me also consider the `uniqueCells` function. It does a `Map`-based dedupe. Could be simpler? The current form is idiomatic and clear.

What about reducing the APP cost of `nextGeneration` by inlining one of the small inner functions? Both `isLive` and `countLiveNeighbors` are used inside `isAliveNextGeneration`. `isLive` is also used in the `survives` check directly. Keeping them as named functions reveals intent — this is Rule 2 territory.

Let me try ONE refactoring that I believe might help: simplify the candidates set construction.

Current:
```typescript
const candidates = uniqueCells([
  ...liveCells,
  ...liveCells.flatMap(neighborsOf),
]);
```

We can note that any live cell is also (by definition) a neighbor of itself if we add itself to NEIGHBOR_OFFSETS... but that would change `neighborsOf` semantics, which is not desirable.

Actually, every live cell IS a neighbor of any neighboring live cell (mutually). So if a live cell has any live neighbors, it appears in `flatMap(neighborsOf)`. But an isolated live cell (no live neighbors) would NOT appear there. So we DO need both.

Wait, actually — an isolated live cell still needs to be considered as a candidate (to die). So we need it. The current code is correct and minimal.

Let me try one different angle: **the cellKey function and uniqueCells dedupe by string keys, but candidates is computed then filtered by `isAliveNextGeneration` which uses `liveSet` (also keyed). Could we avoid the dedupe by using a Map directly throughout?**

Actually, looking again, this would add complexity without measurable benefit since the operation is well-encapsulated.

Given the code is already exceptionally clean — **zero ESLint smells, max McCabe 3, max APP ~30, all functions have clear names** — let me try one experiment to see if any small change improves measurements.

### Iteration 1 — Refactoring Attempt: Inline the named booleans

Let me try inlining and re-measure to see if it improves any metric.

Actually no — I'm going to first consider whether the most useful change might be **reorganizing for clarity** rather than reducing numbers. Let me look once more at the production file with fresh eyes.

Looking at `nextGeneration`, the inner closures (`isLive`, `countLiveNeighbors`, `isAliveNextGeneration`) all close over `liveSet`. This creates implicit coupling. Could pulling them out as pure functions taking `liveSet` as a parameter help?

But that would *increase* APP mass (more parameters, more invocations). And the closures don't hurt clarity — they actually make the dataflow explicit.

Let me try the refactoring that removes the named intermediates and see if it actually improves:

input.command: pnpm exec eslint src/ --format json 2>&1 | tail -50

content.text:
### Iteration 1 — POST Measurements

POST: `isAliveNextGeneration`:
- Bindings: `cell`, `n` = 2
- Assignments: `const n` = 1 → +6
- Invocations: `countLiveNeighbors`, `isLive` = 2 → +4
- Conditionals (`||`, `&&`): 2 → +8
- Comparisons (`===` ×2)
- Mass: ~22 (down from ~30)
- McCabe: 3 (unchanged — still has `||` and `&&`)

**Pre/Post Deltas (function `isAliveNextGeneration`)**:
- ESLint smells: 0 → 0
- APP mass: 30 → 22 (-8)
- McCabe cyclomatic: 3 → 3 (unchanged)

But wait — I traded away two intent-revealing named booleans (`reproduces`, `survives`) for slightly lower APP mass. This violates Rule 2 (Reveals Intent trumps APP). The domain concepts of "reproduction" and "survival" are core to Conway's Game of Life rules. Let me revert.

input.description: Verify tests still pass after revert

content.content:
✓ src/game-of-life.spec.ts  (9 tests) 4ms

 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  9 passed (9)
   Start at  13:17:34
   Duration  178ms (transform 29ms, setup 0ms, collect 25ms, tests 4ms, environment 0ms, prepare 49ms)

content.text:
**Iteration 1 — Reverted**: APP dropped 30→22 but McCabe was unchanged AND the named booleans (`reproduces`, `survives`) directly express Conway's domain rules. Rule 2 (Reveals Intent) trumps APP mass reduction.

## Iteration 2 — Other Refactoring Angles

Let me consider other possibilities:

1. **Extracting `isAliveNextGeneration` and helpers as top-level pure functions** — would lose closure-based clarity, add params, raise APP. No improvement.

2. **Replacing the `||` with a lookup or pattern** — overengineering for 2 rules. Would worsen clarity (Rule 2) and likely raise McCabe (additional structures).

3. **Consolidating `isLive` and `liveSet` checks** — currently `isLive` is a thin wrapper. Could inline it? It's used twice (once explicitly, once via filter). The named function reveals intent. Keep.

4. **Reduce `nextGeneration`'s candidate computation** — current form is already minimal:
   ```typescript
   const candidates = uniqueCells([...liveCells, ...liveCells.flatMap(neighborsOf)]);
   ```
   This is one line, clear, idiomatic.

5. **Combine `cellKey` into `uniqueCells`** — `cellKey` is used in 3 places (uniqueCells, liveSet, isLive). Extracting it is correct (DRY) and reveals intent.

6. **Adjust `NEIGHBOR_OFFSETS` representation** — current grid is visually clear (8 offsets arranged spatially). Changing it would lose visual intent.

Looking at the file again, I see no further improvement opportunities that wouldn't sacrifice clarity. Let me do one final ESLint and measurement to confirm.

input.command: pnpm exec eslint src/ --format json 2>&1 | tail -5

content.text:
## Iteration 2 — No Further Improvement Possible

**Final Whole-src State**:
- ESLint smells: **0**
- Max cognitive complexity: well below thresholds (no SonarJS warning)
- Max APP mass: **30** (in `isAliveNextGeneration`, intentionally retained for domain clarity via `reproduces`/`survives` names)
- Max McCabe cyclomatic: **3** (in `isAliveNextGeneration`, intrinsic to Conway's "reproduce OR survive" rule)
- All 9 tests pass

**Reasoning**:
- Every function has a clear, intent-revealing name
- No cross-file duplication (single production file with cohesive helpers)
- McCabe is at the floor for the algorithm: Conway's rule fundamentally has 2 decision branches (reproduces/survives) plus an `&&` for survival's "currently alive" precondition. Any further reduction would either change semantics or move the decisions elsewhere without removing them.
- The one attempted refactoring (inlining named booleans) reduced APP mass by 8 but sacrificed two intent-revealing domain terms — reverted per Rule 2 priority.

---

# End-Refactor Complete

**Iterations applied**: 0 net (1 attempted, 1 reverted)

**Per-iteration summary**:
1. Inline `reproduces`/`survives` booleans into a single return expression in `isAliveNextGeneration` — smells [0→0], APP [30→22], McCabe [3→3]. **Reverted**: McCabe unchanged and the named booleans express Conway's domain rules; Rule 2 (Reveals Intent) trumps APP reduction.

**Whole-src final state**:
- Total ESLint smells: **0**
- Max cognitive complexity: trivial (no SonarJS warning emitted)
- Max APP mass: **~30** (in `isAliveNextGeneration` at `/home/experimenter/experiments/runs/2026-05-30_12-56-42_game-of-life-example-mapping_v6.5-end-refactor_opus-4-7-no-thinking/src/game-of-life.ts`)
- Max McCabe cyclomatic: **3** (in `isAliveNextGeneration`, intrinsic to the algorithm)

**Tests**: 9/9 passing

**Relevant files**:
- `/home/experimenter/experiments/runs/2026-05-30_12-56-42_game-of-life-example-mapping_v6.5-end-refactor_opus-4-7-no-thinking/src/game-of-life.ts` (production code — unchanged after revert)
- `/home/experimenter/experiments/runs/2026-05-30_12-56-42_game-of-life-example-mapping_v6.5-end-refactor_opus-4-7-no-thinking/src/game-of-life.spec.ts` (tests, all passing)

The code arrived at the end-refactor pass already polished by the per-cycle refactor agent: zero smells, minimal McCabe, intent-revealing names, no cross-file concerns (single-file kata). The measurement-driven loop found no improvement that didn't trade clarity for marginal numbers.
